text.title: Approach Selection: Spectrum, Dimensions, and Hybrid Patterns
text.summary: The predictive-to-adaptive spectrum, six scoring dimensions for approach selection, and named hybrid patterns with their appropriate contexts.

section.title: Selection Dimensions

rule.statement: Score the project on all six dimensions before recommending an approach. Never recommend based on team preference or default to agile without scoring.
rule.rationale: LLMs have a heavy agile bias from training data. Unscored recommendations are PM preference, not project fit. Scoring creates an auditable decision record.

list.item: Requirement clarity — stable and well-defined favours predictive; evolving and emergent favours agile
Stakeholder engagement — limited access favours predictive; highly engaged favours agile
Risk tolerance — low (must work first time) favours predictive; high (can experiment) favours agile
Team agility — less experienced favours predictive; self-organising favours agile
Contract flexibility — fixed price/scope favours predictive; time and materials favours agile
Regulatory burden — high (FDA, DoD, banking phase gates) favours predictive; low favours agile

section.reference: Six-dimension scoring script, outputs recommendation JSON

rule.statement: When recommending "hybrid", always name the specific pattern. Generic "hybrid" in output must be rejected and re-prompted.
rule.rationale: Generic hybrid recommendations produce mixed signals — teams implement waterfall with daily standups (overhead without benefit). A named pattern specifies which phase uses which method.

list.item: Water-Scrum-Fall: Plan phase (predictive) → Build phase (Scrum) → Deploy phase (predictive). Caps agile benefit — total cycle time is still long.
Agile-discovery-then-predictive: Agile discovery phase to clarify requirements → Waterfall implementation once scope is locked. Good for fixed-price contracts with unclear initial scope.
Predictive-governance-over-agile-execution: Phase gates and funding approvals are predictive; sprint execution within each phase is agile. Two reporting cadences — sprint review + phase gate.

example.note: Hybrid for a $100K fixed-price contract over 4 months.
example.code:
Phase 1: Discovery (2 weeks) — Agile - User story workshops, prototype key screens, finalise scope Phase 2: Development (10 weeks) — Scrum - 5 two-week sprints, weekly client demos, backlog refinement Phase 3: Deployment (2 weeks) — Predictive - Fixed test plan, staged rollout, documentation
Governance: - Monthly steering committee (predictive gate) - Weekly sprint reviews (agile cadence) - Change requests required for scope changes (predictive control)

antipattern.description: Agile-in-name-only: daily standups plus a waterfall plan.
antipattern.reason: Standups without a working sprint backlog and empirical planning produce the overhead of both methods with the benefit of neither.

antipattern.description: Recommending agile on a fixed-price contract without a scope-envelope warning.
antipattern.reason: Fixed-price plus agile only works with a scope-envelope contract (range of stories, change budget, exit clauses). Without it, every backlog refinement is a contract dispute.

antipattern.description: Adopting SAFe to fix a single-team velocity problem.
antipattern.reason: SAFe is a multi-team scaling framework. Applying it to a one-team problem adds PI planning, ARTs, and RTEs without solving the velocity root cause.
